abstract:
Nitrate ($NO_{3}^{-}$) contamination in water sources poses a significant threat to global health and ecological stability, necessitating the development of efficient removal technologies. While bimetallic palladium-copper (Pd/Cu) systems are known to facilitate the nitrate reduction reaction (NRR), the comparative performance of Pd single atoms versus nanoparticles across different catalytic regimes remains poorly understood. In this study, we synthesized a library of Pd/Cu catalysts ranging from Pd-rich alloys to Pd/Cu(1:100) single-atom alloys (SAAs) to evaluate their performance in thermocatalytic (T-NRR) versus electrocatalytic (E-NRR) pathways. 

Our results reveal a striking divergence in catalytic capability: while the Pd/Cu(1:100) SAA performed poorly in the thermocatalytic system, it exhibited exceptional activity ($TOF_{Pd}$ = 2 min$^{-1}$) and superior $N_{2}$ selectivity (94%) in the electrocatalytic system, outperforming its nanoalloy counterparts. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations elucidate the mechanistic origins of this contrast, attributing the enhanced E-NRR performance of the SAA to increased $NO_{3}*$ adsorption stability under alkaline electrochemical conditions. Furthermore, the localized pH environment and the ability to utilize water as a proton source in E-NRR lower the kinetic barrier for $N_{2}$ formation relative to ammonia. This work highlights the unique advantages of the SAA architecture for selective electrochemical water treatment and establishes critical design principles for transitioning between thermocatalytic and electrocatalytic processes.
